questions.question: What is this course about?
questions.answer: This 14-week hands-on course teaches computational thinking through building and programming a low-cost wheeled robot. Students program a Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 robot in MicroPython, progressing from blinking LEDs to wirelessly connected swarm robots coordinating over Bluetooth. Every lab reinforces abstraction, algorithm design, decomposition, and pattern recognition.

questions.question: Who is this course designed for?
questions.answer: The course targets grades 8-12 with no prior coding experience required. Only basic keyboard and browser comfort is needed. The robot costs under $35 and requires no soldering, making it accessible to schools with modest budgets.

questions.question: Do I need any previous coding experience?
questions.answer: No. The course starts from the very beginning — variables, loops, functions. No prior Python or MicroPython experience is needed. Students who have coded before will move quickly through early chapters and find new challenges in wireless and swarm robotics units.

questions.question: How much does the robot cost?
questions.answer: The complete robot — Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 board, Smart Car chassis, DC motors, time-of-flight sensor, NeoPixel LEDs, OLED display, piezo buzzer, and battery pack — costs approximately $35 USD. The Raspberry Pi Pico W variant for WiFi/Bluetooth costs a few dollars more.

questions.question: What hardware and software do I need to get started?
questions.answer: Hardware: Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 robot kit, four AA batteries, and a USB cable. Software: the free Thonny IDE. The robot runs MicroPython firmware installed once via a UF2 file — no separate Python installation needed.

questions.question: Is soldering required to build the robot?
questions.answer: No. This is an explicit design goal. All components connect using Grove connectors and Dupont jumper wires — plug-and-play connections. No soldering iron, flux, or heat is needed, making the course safe for any classroom.

questions.question: What is a MicroSim?
questions.answer: A MicroSim is an interactive browser-based simulation embedded in the textbook. MicroSims let you experiment with concepts — adjusting PWM duty cycles, watching H-bridge states, exploring collision avoidance logic — without needing the physical robot. There are over 20 MicroSims in this textbook.

questions.question: What programming language does this course use?
questions.answer: All robot programming is done in MicroPython, a compact version of Python 3 designed for microcontrollers. Syntax is nearly identical to standard Python, so skills transfer directly. The course uses the free Thonny IDE.

questions.question: How long does the course take?
questions.answer: The course is 14 weeks, typically meeting two to three times per week. Each week introduces one major topic through reading, MicroSims, and hands-on labs. The final two weeks are reserved for student-designed capstone projects.

questions.question: What is the capstone project?
questions.answer: An open-ended student-designed project (Weeks 13-14) that must integrate at least two sensor types, a display, and a novel algorithm. Students document their design, present the robot, and write a reflection connecting the project to the four pillars of computational thinking.

questions.question: What is computational thinking?
questions.answer: Computational thinking is a problem-solving methodology applying computer science techniques to complex problems. It consists of four core skills: decomposition (breaking problems into parts), abstraction (focusing on what matters), algorithm design (step-by-step solutions), and pattern recognition (identifying reusable similarities). It is the lens through every robot challenge in this course.

questions.question: What are the four pillars of computational thinking?
questions.answer: 1. Decomposition — breaking complex problems into smaller pieces. 2. Abstraction — hiding complexity behind a name (e.g., calling drive_forward() without thinking about pin numbers). 3. Algorithm Design — writing a precise ordered sequence of steps. 4. Pattern Recognition — noticing repeating structures you can reuse across programs.

questions.question: What is physical computing?
questions.answer: Physical computing is the practice of using hardware and software together to sense the physical world via inputs (sensors, buttons) and affect it via outputs (motors, LEDs, speakers). Your robot is a physical computing device: programs read distance from a sensor, make a decision, and drive motors in response.

questions.question: What is MicroPython?
questions.answer: MicroPython is a lean implementation of Python 3 that runs directly on microcontrollers. It gives standard Python syntax in a form small enough for 264 KB of RAM. On this robot it provides built-in modules for GPIO, PWM, I2C, SPI, and Bluetooth. Thonny IDE connects over USB for writing, uploading, and running programs.

questions.question: What is the Cytron Maker Pi RP2040?
questions.answer: The Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 is the robot's brain — a PCB built around the Raspberry Pi RP2040 microcontroller. It includes two H-bridge motor drivers, four NeoPixel LEDs, a piezo buzzer, Grove connectors, and a battery holder. It costs roughly $12-$15 USD.

questions.question: What is a GPIO pin?
questions.answer: GPIO (General-Purpose Input/Output) pins are numbered electrical connection points on the microcontroller that programs can control. An output pin can be driven HIGH (3.3V) or LOW (0V) for LEDs and motor drivers. An input pin reads whether an external signal is HIGH or LOW for buttons and digital sensors.

questions.question: What is PWM?
questions.answer: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) simulates a variable voltage using rapid on/off switching. The duty cycle — the fraction of time spent HIGH — controls delivered power. 50% duty cycle drives a motor at roughly half speed; 100% is full speed; 0% stops it. PWM controls motor speed, servo angle, LED brightness, and speaker tone.

questions.question: What is an H-bridge circuit?
questions.answer: An H-bridge is a circuit of four transistor switches arranged in an H shape that allows current to flow through a motor in either direction — enabling forward, reverse, or stop. The Cytron board includes two built-in H-bridge motor driver ICs, one per wheel, so no manual wiring of the H-bridge is needed.

questions.question: What is differential drive?
questions.answer: Differential drive is the steering mechanism for two-wheeled robots: speed each wheel independently to steer. Equal speed = straight. Right wheel faster = curves left. Left forward, right backward = pivot in place. All turning in this course uses differential drive — no separate steering mechanism exists.

questions.question: What is a time-of-flight sensor?
questions.answer: A time-of-flight (ToF) sensor measures distance by emitting a laser pulse and timing how long the reflection takes to return. The VL53L0X on this robot measures 3-200 cm with millimeter precision over I2C. ToF sensors are more accurate than ultrasonic sensors and work well indoors.

questions.question: What is the I2C bus?
questions.answer: I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) is a two-wire serial protocol (SDA data + SCL clock) that lets a microcontroller talk to multiple sensors and displays. Each device has a unique address. On this robot, I2C connects the VL53L0X ToF sensor and SSD1306 OLED display on GPIO pins 16 (SDA) and 17 (SCL).

questions.question: What is collision avoidance?
questions.answer: Collision avoidance is a robot behavior that uses closed-loop feedback: the ToF sensor continuously measures distance, the program compares to a threshold, and motors respond — stop, turn randomly, then drive forward again. It is one of the first complete autonomous behaviors students implement.

questions.question: What is line following?
questions.answer: Line following uses two infrared sensors under the robot to detect a dark line on a light floor. Each sensor outputs digital HIGH or LOW. The program reads both sensors and adjusts each wheel's speed via motor differential — if the left sensor leaves the line, the left wheel slows to steer back.

questions.question: What is closed-loop feedback?
questions.answer: Closed-loop feedback is a control strategy where the robot continuously measures the result of its actions and adjusts based on what it measures — unlike open-loop where commands are sent without checking outcomes. Collision avoidance, line following, and servo positioning all use closed-loop feedback.

questions.question: What is Bluetooth Low Energy?
questions.answer: BLE is a wireless standard designed for short-range, low-power data transfer — ideal for robot commands. The Raspberry Pi Pico W includes a BLE radio. In this course, BLE enables direct robot-to-robot communication without a WiFi router. Two robots can discover, pair, and exchange movement commands in real time.

questions.question: What is swarm robotics?
questions.answer: Swarm robotics studies how groups of simple robots exhibit complex collective behavior through local interactions — without central coordination. Each robot follows simple rules, but the group outcome (collective obstacle avoidance, convoy following, synchronized movement) is more sophisticated than any single robot could achieve.

questions.question: What is emergent behavior in robots?
questions.answer: Emergent behavior occurs when simple per-robot rules produce complex, unplanned patterns at the group level — like a school of fish with no leader. In this course, swarm robots exhibit emergent behavior when rules like 'mirror the leader's speed' or 'stop if your neighbor stops' create flock-like collective motion.

questions.question: What is a PWM duty cycle?
questions.answer: The duty cycle is the percentage of time a PWM signal is HIGH. On the RP2040 it is expressed as a 16-bit integer from 0 (always off) to 65535 (always on). 32767 (~50%) drives a motor at roughly half speed. Motors also have a dead zone — a minimum duty cycle below which they don't spin.

questions.question: What does the config.py file do?
questions.answer: config.py centralizes all hardware constants — GPIO pin numbers, PWM frequencies, I2C bus IDs, sensor addresses, and speed limits. Every other program imports with 'from config import *' so changing hardware means updating one file. This is called the import config pattern and is a fundamental software engineering practice.

questions.question: What is a secrets.py file?
questions.answer: secrets.py stores sensitive credentials — WiFi SSID and password — separately from the main program. It is listed in .gitignore so it is never committed to a public repository. Programs import credentials with 'from secrets import SSID, PASSWORD'. This keeps code shareable while credentials stay private.

questions.question: What is the difference between a for loop and a while loop?
questions.answer: A for loop runs a fixed number of times iterating over a sequence. A while loop runs as long as a condition is true — potentially forever. Robot main loops almost always use 'while True:' because the robot needs to keep responding indefinitely. For loops handle fixed-count tasks like LED animations.

questions.question: What is exception handling in MicroPython?
questions.answer: Exception handling uses try/except/finally blocks to catch errors and run cleanup code even when something goes wrong. The finally block ALWAYS runs — even after KeyboardInterrupt (Ctrl+C) — ensuring motors are safely stopped instead of running forever after a program exits.

questions.question: What is the difference between I2C and SPI?
questions.answer: I2C uses two wires (SDA + SCL) and supports multiple devices by address — slower but cable-efficient. SPI uses four wires and is faster, but each device needs its own chip-select wire. On this robot, I2C connects the ToF sensor and OLED display; SPI is available for high-speed peripherals.

questions.question: What is a framebuffer?
questions.answer: A framebuffer is a region of RAM holding pixel data for one complete display frame. Instead of sending each drawing command directly to the screen (causing flicker), you draw everything into the framebuffer, then call .show() to push the entire buffer to the display in one operation for flicker-free output.

questions.question: What are GATT, central, and peripheral in BLE?
questions.answer: GATT (Generic Attribute Profile) defines how BLE data is organized. A GATT service is a logical data grouping; a characteristic is one specific value within it. A peripheral advertises and waits for connections (typically the robot being controlled). A central scans and connects (typically the controller).

questions.question: What is the difference between open-loop and closed-loop motor control?
questions.answer: Open-loop sends a fixed command without checking the result — simple but inaccurate due to friction, voltage, and surface variation. Closed-loop continuously measures the outcome and adjusts. Collision avoidance reads the sensor every iteration and changes speed based on measured distance — more complex but far more reliable.

questions.question: What is the difference between BLE and WiFi for robot control?
questions.answer: WiFi provides higher bandwidth, longer range (50+ meters), and browser control via any device on the network. BLE has shorter range (10-30 meters), lower power, and enables direct robot-to-robot communication without a router. WiFi suits browser dashboards; BLE suits swarms and peer-to-peer scenarios.

questions.question: What is HTTP GET vs. POST?
questions.answer: HTTP GET retrieves information — loading the robot's control web page in the browser. HTTP POST sends data to trigger an action — clicking a Forward button sends a POST with the command. POST is safer for state-changing actions because browsers don't cache or pre-fetch POST requests.

questions.question: What is a UF2 firmware file?
questions.answer: A UF2 file is a binary format for flashing firmware onto microcontrollers like the Raspberry Pi Pico. Hold BOOTSEL while plugging in USB and the Pico appears as a USB drive. Drag-drop the rp2-pico-micropython.uf2 file onto it, and the Pico automatically flashes MicroPython and reboots.

questions.question: What does the `machine` module do?
questions.answer: The machine module is MicroPython's hardware abstraction layer. It provides Pin (GPIO), PWM (pulse width modulation), I2C, SPI, Timer, and more. Virtually every robot program in this course imports from machine. Without it you cannot control any hardware on the RP2040.

questions.question: What is the REPL?
questions.answer: The REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) is an interactive command-line interface where you type MicroPython commands and see results immediately. In Thonny, the REPL panel lets you test code snippets on the robot without writing a full program — essential for checking sensor readings and testing motor commands.

questions.question: What is NeoPixel and how do I control it?
questions.answer: NeoPixel is Adafruit's name for WS2812B addressable RGB LEDs where each LED has its own control chip settable to any of 16 million colors. The Cytron board has four NeoPixels. The MicroPython neopixel module lets you set any LED's red, green, blue channels as integers 0-255 independently.

questions.question: What is the BOOTSEL button?
questions.answer: The BOOTSEL (Boot Selection) button on the Raspberry Pi Pico puts the board into USB mass storage mode when held during USB connection — allowing you to drag a UF2 firmware file onto it. Under normal operation (not held), the Pico boots directly into MicroPython. You only need it during initial setup.

questions.question: What is a potentiometer input?
questions.answer: A potentiometer is a variable resistor (a knob) that outputs a voltage proportional to its position. On the robot it connects to an ADC pin, reading a value 0-65535 corresponding to position. Potentiometers adjust parameters at runtime — tuning collision threshold or LED brightness — without re-uploading code.

questions.question: What is the gitignore file for?
questions.answer: The .gitignore file tells Git which files to exclude from commits. In this course it must include secrets.py to prevent WiFi credentials from being pushed to a public GitHub repository. It may also exclude __pycache__ directories and other generated files.

questions.question: Why won't my robot move when I run my program?
questions.answer: Check: 1) Battery charged? Motors need more current than sensors — weak batteries keep the OLED lit but not motors. 2) USB cable — some are charge-only. 3) Pin assignments in config.py match physical wiring. 4) Duty cycle above the dead zone (~15000-20000). 5) Run motor connection test lab first.

questions.question: Why does my distance sensor always read 0?
questions.answer: Check: 1) Wiring — SDA and SCL on correct pins, VCC and GND connected. 2) Run I2C scanner to verify device appears at address 0x29. 3) vl53l0x.py library uploaded to robot flash. 4) Sensor too close (<3cm) or too far (>200cm). 5) Zero-distance calibration may be needed.

questions.question: Why does my robot drive in circles?
questions.answer: DC motors of the same model have slightly different friction and winding characteristics, so identical duty cycles produce different speeds. Fix: add a small trim constant to one motor's duty cycle in config.py. Also check motor wiring connections and wheel seating on shafts.

questions.question: My motors keep running after I press Ctrl+C. How do I stop them?
questions.answer: This happens without a try/except/finally block. PWM signals keep running after Python stops. The fix: wrap the main loop in 'try: main_loop() finally: stop_motors()'. The finally block runs even on Ctrl+C and sets all motor duty cycles to 0.

questions.question: My WiFi won't connect. What should I check?
questions.answer: Check: 1) SSID and PASSWORD in secrets.py match exactly (case-sensitive). 2) Using 2.4 GHz network — Pico W does not support 5 GHz. 3) Standard WPA2 network — not enterprise authentication. 4) Run ping test slow lab first. 5) Connection loop waits 5-15 seconds — WiFi takes time.

questions.question: Thonny says 'No module named X'. How do I fix it?
questions.answer: The library file is not in the robot's flash storage. Fix: 1) In Thonny, go to View > Files. 2) Navigate to the library file on your computer (left panel). 3) Right-click > Upload to / to copy it to the robot. 4) Verify it appears in the right panel. 5) Re-run your program.

questions.question: Why is my OLED display showing nothing?
questions.answer: Check: 1) I2C wiring — SDA and SCL on GPIO 16 and 17. 2) Run I2C scanner to confirm display at address 0x3C or 0x3D. 3) Missing .show() call after drawing — the framebuffer is invisible until flushed. 4) ssd1306.py library must be uploaded to the robot.

questions.question: My BLE robots won't find each other. What should I check?
questions.answer: Check: 1) Both programs running simultaneously on separate Pico W boards. 2) Peripheral's advertised name matches what the central scans for. 3) Robots within 1-2 meters to start. 4) Previous session may not have disconnected — restart both robots. 5) Watch REPL output for connection status messages.

questions.question: Why does my servo motor jitter?
questions.answer: Common causes: 1) Wrong PWM frequency — servos need 50 Hz. 2) Pulse width outside valid range (500-2500 µs). 3) Weak batteries causing voltage drops on startup. 4) Calibration constants in config.py need adjustment for your servo's actual min/max pulse widths.

questions.question: My program runs but the robot doesn't behave as expected. Where do I start?
questions.answer: Use systematic debugging: 1) Isolate subsystems — test motors alone, sensor alone. 2) Add print statements to observe values in the REPL. 3) Check logic flow with 'print(reached if branch)'. 4) Simplify by commenting out complex features. 5) Distinguish hardware bugs (wiring) from software bugs (logic).

questions.question: Why does my line follower lose the line on curves?
questions.answer: Motor differential adjustment may be too slow or small. Fix: 1) Increase speed differential on curve detection for more aggressive correction. 2) Lower overall speed for more reaction time. 3) Reduce sleep delay between sensor reads to 10-20 ms. 4) Check sensor mounting height — 3-8 mm above surface.

questions.question: Why should I always use a config.py file?
questions.answer: config.py makes code portable (update hardware, change one file), readable (LEFT_FORWARD_PIN instead of raw number 17), and shareable (others can use your program by updating config.py). It implements the hardware abstraction layer pattern used in professional embedded systems development.

questions.question: How should I organize a robot program?
questions.answer: Use the four-section structure from this course: 1) Imports and config. 2) Hardware initialization (PWM objects, I2C, sensors). 3) Helper functions (drive_forward, stop_motors, read_distance). 4) Main loop (try: while True: [logic] finally: stop_motors()). This structure makes programs readable, testable, and extensible.

questions.question: Why must I use try/except/finally in robot programs?
questions.answer: Motors continue running until their duty cycle is set to zero — they don't stop just because the program ends. Without finally, Ctrl+C leaves motors spinning and the robot drives away. The finally block guarantees cleanup code runs regardless of how the program exits: normal exit, Ctrl+C, or any error.

questions.question: How do I calibrate a sensor properly?
questions.answer: Two-point calibration: 1) Zero point — hold sensor at known reference, record raw reading as ZERO_DISTANCE_OFFSET. 2) Scale point — hold at known distance (100mm), compute SCALE_FACTOR = 100 / (raw - offset). 3) Apply: calibrated = (raw - offset) * scale. 4) Validate at several distances. Store in config.py.

questions.question: Should I use WiFi or BLE for my robot project?
questions.answer: WiFi when: browser control from phone/laptop, data logging to a server, or range > 30m. BLE when: direct robot-to-robot communication, building a swarm, low power needed, or no WiFi router available. Both use the Raspberry Pi Pico W.

questions.question: How do I write readable MicroPython code for robots?
questions.answer: Best practices: name constants ALL_CAPS (MAX_SPEED = 60000), name functions with verbs (drive_forward, stop_motors), keep functions under 15 lines, use config.py for all pin numbers, add comments only where the WHY is non-obvious, and test incrementally (motors before sensors before collision logic).

questions.question: How should I safely test a new robot program?
questions.answer: 1) Test on desk first — prop the robot up so wheels spin in air. 2) Start at 20-30% duty cycle for new motor code. 3) Keep your hand near the USB cable to cut power instantly. 4) Test in a clear area. 5) Add a wall (book) for collision avoidance testing — not a glass shelf.

questions.question: How do I choose the right sensor for my project?
questions.answer: Time-of-flight (VL53L0X): accurate distance 3-200cm, narrow beam. Ultrasonic: wide beam 2-400cm, slower. Infrared: line detection and close proximity, affected by light and surface color. Bump switch: hard contact only. Potentiometer: manual parameter tuning. For collision avoidance use ToF; for line following use IR.

questions.question: How do I use Git effectively for robot projects?
questions.answer: Key practices: 1) Create .gitignore that excludes secrets.py. 2) Commit after each working milestone with meaningful messages. 3) Never commit WiFi passwords. 4) Use GitHub to back up work. 5) Write messages like 'Add collision avoidance with 20cm threshold' not just 'update'.

questions.question: When should I use open-loop vs. closed-loop motor control?
questions.answer: Open-loop: when exact positioning doesn't matter (dance sequences, demos, testing). Closed-loop: when the robot must respond to real-world variation — collision avoidance, line following, target distance maintenance. Closed-loop is more robust against motor wear, battery voltage, and surface friction.

questions.question: What is a state machine pattern?
questions.answer: A state machine models a system as discrete states (IDLE, DRIVING, AVOIDING, TURNING) with defined transitions. The robot stores its current state in a variable; the main loop executes behavior for the current state and transitions when conditions are met. State machines make complex behaviors easier to reason about and extend than nested if-statements.

questions.question: How does PID control work?
questions.answer: PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) computes corrections using three components: P (proportional to current error), I (proportional to accumulated past error — removes steady-state offset), D (proportional to rate of error change — dampens oscillation). PID produces smoother control than on/off logic — e.g., a PID line follower adjusts motor differential gradually.

questions.question: What is multithreading on a microcontroller?
questions.answer: The RP2040 has two processor cores, so MicroPython can run two threads via the _thread module. Example: one thread reads sensors continuously while another handles web server requests or BLE messages. Shared data must be protected with locks to prevent race conditions. asyncio is often preferred for simpler coordination.

questions.question: How do I design a custom swarm behavior?
questions.answer: Four steps: 1) Define the goal (convoy, collective obstacle avoidance, synchronized dance). 2) Write individual rules (what each robot senses and does). 3) Define BLE messages (what data robots exchange and how often). 4) Test with two robots first, then scale. Key insight: program per-robot rules, not the group behavior directly.

questions.question: What is sensor fusion?
questions.answer: Sensor fusion combines readings from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate or reliable measurement than any single sensor provides. For example, combining ToF, ultrasonic, and IR data gives a more complete picture of the robot's surroundings. Simple fusion is averaging; advanced methods use Kalman filters weighting sensors by accuracy.

questions.question: What is asynchronous programming in MicroPython?
questions.answer: asyncio lets multiple tasks interleave without threads. Async functions yield control with 'await asyncio.sleep_ms()' instead of blocking, allowing another coroutine to run. Useful for handling BLE events and sensor reading simultaneously in a single-threaded style. asyncio is more predictable than _thread for most robot coordination needs.

questions.question: What are the physical limitations of this robot?
questions.answer: Key limits: max speed ~0.5 m/s, ToF sensor reliable 3-200 cm, WiFi range ~30m indoors, BLE range ~10-20m indoors, AA battery life 1-3 hours depending on motor use, 2 MB flash storage, 264 KB RAM. Plan capstone projects with these constraints in mind.

questions.question: What topics are NOT covered in this course?
questions.answer: Explicitly out of scope: cloud IoT platforms (AWS, Azure), TinyML, computer vision, GPS/outdoor navigation, SLAM, soldering/custom PCBs, ROS, 3D printing, MQTT/REST APIs (brief overview only). These are excellent directions for independent study after completing this course.

questions.question: What is the engineering design process?
questions.answer: The engineering design process is an iterative plan-build-test-improve cycle. In robotics: design a solution on paper, write code, run it on the robot, observe what goes wrong, and refine. Students apply this in every lab — adjusting collision thresholds, sensor calibration values, and line-following gains through deliberate iteration.

questions.question: What is a servo motor?
questions.answer: A servo motor rotates to a specific angle and holds it, unlike a DC motor that spins continuously. Servos are controlled by PWM: a 50 Hz signal whose pulse width tells the servo which angle to hold (typically 0-180°). On this robot a servo can mount the ToF sensor to sweep and scan the environment.

questions.question: What is an OLED display?
questions.answer: The robot's OLED display is a 128x64 pixel monochrome screen showing text, geometric shapes, bar charts, and animations. It uses the SSD1306 driver over I2C. Programs draw to a framebuffer in RAM then call .show() to flush the frame to the screen — fast and flicker-free.

questions.question: What is WiFi IoT control?
questions.answer: WiFi IoT control lets you control the robot from a web browser over a local wireless network. A Raspberry Pi Pico W acts as a web server: connects to WiFi, gets an IP address, and serves an HTML control page. Clicking a button in the browser sends an HTTP request that drives the motors.

questions.question: Who is Sparky?
questions.answer: Sparky is the course mascot — a cartoon version of the two-wheeled robot you build. Sparky appears in exactly six roles per chapter: welcoming you at the start, helping you think through hard concepts, sharing practical tips, warning about common pitfalls, encouraging you when things are difficult, and celebrating at chapter end.

questions.question: How is this textbook organized?
questions.answer: 13 chapters follow a deliberate learning graph where each builds on the previous: computational thinking (Ch 1), hardware assembly (Ch 2), MicroPython setup (Ch 3), programming fundamentals (Ch 4-5), electronics and motors (Ch 6-7), sensors (Ch 8), displays (Ch 9), robot behaviors (Ch 10), WiFi (Ch 11), BLE (Ch 12), swarm robotics (Ch 13).

questions.question: What does the `time` module do?
questions.answer: The time module provides sleep functions (time.sleep() and time.sleep_ms()) for pausing execution, and time.ticks_ms() / time.ticks_diff() for measuring elapsed time without blocking. In robot programs, time.sleep_ms(100) creates a 100ms pause between sensor readings for stable polling.

questions.question: What is the VL53L0X sensor?
questions.answer: The VL53L0X is the specific time-of-flight distance sensor used in this course, made by ST Microelectronics. It uses a 940nm laser to measure 3-200cm distances, communicates over I2C at address 0x29, and requires the vl53l0x.py library. Raw readings need zero-distance offset and scale factor calibration.

questions.question: What is the SSD1306 display driver?
questions.answer: The SSD1306 is the controller chip inside the robot's OLED display. The MicroPython ssd1306 library provides a SSD1306_I2C class with methods: .text(), .line(), .rect(), .fill_rect(), .ellipse(). Call .show() to flush the framebuffer to screen. Pixel (0,0) is at the top-left of the 128x64 display.

questions.question: What is a microcontroller?
questions.answer: A microcontroller is a small, self-contained computer on a single chip with a processor, RAM, flash memory, and I/O peripherals. Unlike a general-purpose computer, it runs one dedicated program and controls hardware directly. The RP2040 (Raspberry Pi's chip) runs at up to 133 MHz with 264 KB RAM and controls all robot subsystems.

questions.question: What is the best way to debug a malfunctioning robot?
questions.answer: Systematic process: 1) Reproduce the problem consistently. 2) Isolate subsystems (motor, sensor, display one at a time). 3) Add print statements to observe values in the REPL. 4) Form a hypothesis before making changes. 5) Change one thing at a time. 6) Distinguish hardware (wiring) from software (logic) bugs.

questions.question: How do I reconnect Thonny after my robot crashes?
questions.answer: 1) Press the STOP button (red square) in Thonny to release the serial port. 2) If that fails, close and reopen Thonny. 3) Check the correct COM port or /dev/tty device is selected in the bottom-right corner. 4) If the robot is in a crash loop, hold BOOTSEL while plugging in to enter bootloader mode, then re-flash MicroPython.

questions.question: What is data logging and why use it?
questions.answer: Data logging records sensor readings to flash memory or serial for later analysis. For example, logging distance every 100ms during a test run lets you graph the robot's path and identify where collision avoidance triggered. On the RP2040, log to a CSV file using open() in write mode. Logging turns 'it seemed to work' into measurable evidence for algorithm tuning.

questions.question: What is a servo motor?
questions.answer: A servo rotates to a specific angle and holds it, controlled by 50 Hz PWM with pulse width 500-2500 microseconds mapping to 0-180 degrees. Unlike DC motors (which spin continuously), servos are used for precise positioning — mounting a sensor for a sweep scan, or driving a steering mechanism.

questions.question: What is the Thonny IDE?
questions.answer: Thonny is a free, lightweight Python IDE designed for beginners with a built-in MicroPython mode. It connects to the robot over USB, letting you write code on your computer, run it on the robot immediately, and see output in the REPL shell. Thonny also manages uploading files to the robot's flash storage via its Files panel.
